threats.title: Reentrancy in executeFeeSwap via Malicious userToken Drains validatorToken Reserves
threats.hypothesis_description: executeFeeSwap performs IERC20(userToken).transferFrom before updating pool.reserveUserToken and pool.reserveValidatorToken. If userToken is a malicious/ERC-777 token controlled by the attacker, the callback during transferFrom allows recursive reentry into executeFeeSwap. Since reserveValidatorToken has not been decremented by any prior call in the reentrancy chain, each reentrant invocation passes the sufficiency check and sends out amountOut of real validatorTokens. The attacker drains the entire validatorToken reserve while the malicious token sends nothing (or minimal tokens) on reentrant calls.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code clearly shows external call before state update on lines ~119-124. No reentrancy guard (nonReentrant) is present anywhere in the contract. The attacker controls the userToken contract and can create pools permissionlessly.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(userToken).transferFrom at line ~119 executes BEFORE pool.reserveUserToken += and pool.reserveValidatorToken -= at lines ~121-122, and BEFORE IERC20(validatorToken).transfer at line ~124

preconditions: Attacker deploys a malicious ERC-777/callback-enabled token as userToken
Pool exists with real validatorToken (e.g., USDC) and the malicious userToken, with liquidity from other LPs
No reentrancy guard on executeFeeSwap (verified: none exists)
validatorToken is a standard ERC-20 that successfully transfers

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: external call (transferFrom) before state update (reserve increment/decrement)
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: pool.reserveValidatorToken not decremented before userToken.transferFrom callback; attacker can recursively drain validatorToken
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No ReentrancyGuard / nonReentrant modifier; no checks-effects-interactions pattern

analysis_questions: Can a malicious userToken's transferFrom callback successfully reenter executeFeeSwap multiple times?
How many recursive calls are possible before gas runs out (stack depth limit ~1024)?
Does the uint128 arithmetic revert on the final state update when nested calls unwind?
validation_requirements: Deploy malicious ERC-777-like token with transferFrom callback
Create pool and seed with real validatorToken
Execute reentrancy attack and measure extracted validatorTokens vs deposited

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deploys malicious userToken with callback hook. Creates pool (maliciousToken/USDC). LPs deposit USDC. Attacker calls executeFeeSwap; during transferFrom, malicious token reenters executeFeeSwap N times. Each call extracts amountOut USDC from unchanged reserves. Total drain: N * amountOut, limited only by pool's USDC reserve and gas.
prerequisites: Pool with real validatorToken liquidity from other LPs

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete drainage of all validatorToken reserves in affected pools
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All LPs who deposited validatorToken to pools using malicious userToken
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None - funds irrecoverably transferred to attacker

threats.test_coverage_gap: No reentrancy tests for executeFeeSwap with callback-enabled tokens

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: ~30K gas per recursive call; estimate max recursion depth
technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Minimal - attacker deploys worthless token, needs gas only
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable via internal transaction traces showing recursive executeFeeSwap calls

threats.title: mint() Reentrancy via validatorToken Callback Mints Excess LP Tokens Against Stale Pool State
threats.hypothesis_description: mint() calls IERC20(validatorToken).transferFrom (line ~197) BEFORE updating pool.reserveValidatorToken (line ~200), totalSupply, and liquidityBalances (lines ~202-203). If validatorToken has a callback hook (ERC-777 tokensToSend), the attacker can reenter mint() during the transfer. The inner mint() calculates liquidity using the stale (lower) reserveValidatorToken in the denominator, yielding more LP tokens than warranted. After both calls complete, the attacker holds a disproportionate share of LP tokens, diluting existing LPs.
threats.investigation_rationale: The data flow analysis confirms transferFrom before state update in mint. The liquidity calculation for subsequent deposits uses denom = reserveValidatorToken + (N * reserveUserToken / SCALE). A stale lower reserveValidatorToken means smaller denom and more LP tokens per deposit.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(validatorToken).transferFrom at line ~197 before pool.reserveValidatorToken += at line ~200 and totalSupply += at line ~202

preconditions: validatorToken is ERC-777 or has tokensToSend/tokensReceived callback
Pool already has existing liquidity (not first deposit, so subsequent-deposit branch is taken)
Attacker can split deposit into outer + inner reentrant calls
No reentrancy guard on mint (verified: none exists)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: transferFrom external call before reserve and supply state updates
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Liquidity calculation uses stale reserveValidatorToken in denominator; inner reentrant mint gets more LP tokens
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No nonReentrant modifier; no pre/post balance verification

analysis_questions: How much excess LP token can the attacker mint compared to a normal two-step deposit?
Does the inner mint's state update change the outer mint's calculation on return?
Can this be combined with burn to extract real tokens at inflated LP share?
validation_requirements: Deploy ERC-777 validatorToken with callback on transferFrom
Create pool, add initial liquidity, then perform reentrant mint
Compare LP tokens minted vs expected for equivalent single deposit

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker calls mint(amount1) with ERC-777 validatorToken. During transferFrom, callback reenters mint(amount2). Inner mint calculates liquidity using stale reserves (lower denominator), getting excess LP tokens. Both calls complete, attacker holds more LP share than proportional. Attacker burns LP tokens to extract more than deposited, diluting other LPs.
prerequisites: Existing pool with other LPs' liquidity

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: LP dilution; attacker extracts more tokens on burn than deposited, at expense of other LPs
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Existing LPs in the affected pool
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Damage is permanent once LP tokens are minted disproportionately

threats.test_coverage_gap: No reentrancy tests for mint with callback tokens

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Requires validatorToken deposits (recovered via burn exploit)
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable via nested mint internal transactions

threats.title: Fee-on-Transfer Token Accounting Mismatch Causes Progressive Pool Insolvency
threats.hypothesis_description: All deposit functions (executeFeeSwap, rebalanceSwap, mint) credit the full nominal amount to internal reserves rather than measuring the actual balance change. With fee-on-transfer tokens, the contract receives fewer tokens than the nominal amount, causing reserves to systematically exceed actual balances. Over time, the pool becomes insolvent: later LPs and swappers cannot withdraw because the contract tries to transfer more tokens than it holds. Early participants who detect this can extract real value before the deficit becomes apparent.
threats.investigation_rationale: Lines ~121 (executeFeeSwap: reserveUserToken += amountIn), ~148 (rebalanceSwap: reserveValidatorToken += amountIn), and ~200 (mint: reserveValidatorToken += amountValidatorToken) all use the nominal input amount, never checking actual received balance.
deployment_assumptions: Pool must include a fee-on-transfer token (e.g., STA, PAXG, deflationary tokens)
threats.config_evidence: No token whitelist exists; contract is permissionless; no balance-before/after measurement in any function

proof_of_signal.pattern: pool.reserve += nominal_amount without checking balanceOf before/after transfer

preconditions: Pool is created with a fee-on-transfer token as userToken or validatorToken
Multiple users interact with the pool over time, accumulating accounting deficit
No mechanism exists to reconcile reserves with actual balances

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Reserve accounting uses nominal transfer amounts, not actual received amounts
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: reserves > actual balanceOf after each fee-on-transfer deposit; deficit grows linearly with transaction count
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No balance-before/after measurement; no SafeERC20; no token type validation

analysis_questions: What fee-on-transfer tokens exist on the target deployment chain?
How quickly does the deficit grow to cause burn/swap reverts?
Can an early LP strategically exit before insolvency while others are trapped?
validation_requirements: Create pool with simulated 2% fee-on-transfer token
Execute multiple deposits and swaps, track reserve vs balance divergence
Verify last LP's burn reverts due to insufficient actual balance

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Pool created with fee-on-transfer token. Each deposit credits more to reserves than actually received. After many transactions, reserveToken exceeds actual balance by significant amount. Early LPs burn and receive their full calculated share (contract still has enough). Last LPs' burns revert as contract tries to transfer more than it holds. Early exiters profit at expense of later ones.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Last LPs lose entire deposited value; funds permanently locked in insolvent pool
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Later LPs who cannot withdraw due to insufficient contract balance
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: No recovery mechanism exists; trapped funds are unrecoverable

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitorable by comparing contract balanceOf vs sum of pool reserves

threats.title: burn() Transfers Zero userToken When Pool Has No userToken Reserves, Potentially Reverting and Locking LP Funds
threats.hypothesis_description: When a pool has zero reserveUserToken (e.g., no fee swaps have occurred yet, or all userTokens have been rebalanced out), burn() calculates amountUserToken = liquidity * 0 / totalSupply = 0 and calls IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, 0). Some ERC-20 implementations revert on zero-value transfers (e.g., USDT on certain chains, BNB). If this reverts, the entire burn transaction fails, permanently locking the LP's funds since they cannot withdraw. There is no alternative withdrawal path.
threats.investigation_rationale: The burn function always transfers both tokens regardless of calculated amounts. Lines ~243-244 show both transfers happen unconditionally. A zero-value transfer revert blocks the entire withdrawal.
deployment_assumptions: userToken must be a token that reverts on zero-value transfer
threats.config_evidence: No check for zero amount before transfer at FeeAMM.sol lines 243-244; some tokens (USDT, BNB) are known to revert on zero transfers

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, amountUserToken) called unconditionally; amountUserToken can be 0 when reserveUserToken is 0

preconditions: Pool has zero or negligible reserveUserToken (common state: pool just created, no fee swaps yet)
userToken reverts on zero-value transfer (e.g., USDT on some chains)
LP wants to withdraw via burn()

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unconditional transfer of both tokens without zero-amount check
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: amountUserToken = liquidity * reserveUserToken / totalSupply = 0 when reserveUserToken = 0; transfer(to, 0) reverts on some tokens
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No conditional check: if (amountUserToken > 0) before transfer

analysis_questions: Which target-chain tokens revert on zero-value transfer?
Can the LP work around this by doing a tiny executeFeeSwap first to make reserveUserToken > 0?
Is this a permanent lock or can the LP recover via other means?
validation_requirements: Test burn() with a token that reverts on zero transfer when reserveUserToken = 0
Verify LP funds are permanently locked
Check if any workaround exists

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Legitimate pool created with USDT as userToken. LPs deposit validatorToken. Before any fee swaps occur (reserveUserToken = 0), an LP tries to burn. burn() calls USDT.transfer(to, 0) which reverts. LP funds are locked. Even if someone later does a feeSwap, the dust amounts from rounding could still produce zero for small burns.
prerequisites: Token that reverts on zero transfer
Pool state where one reserve is zero

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: LP funds permanently locked in pool until reserveUserToken becomes non-zero
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All LPs in pools with zero userToken reserves and zero-revert userToken
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Partial - someone could do a minimal feeSwap to make reserveUserToken > 0, enabling burns

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for burn with zero reserves or zero-revert tokens

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: None - this is a DoS condition
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Occurs naturally when no fee swaps have happened
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by checking pool reserve state before burn attempt

threats.title: executeFeeSwap Accepts Zero amountIn, Enabling No-Cost Event Emission and Potential Token Interaction Side Effects
threats.hypothesis_description: executeFeeSwap does not validate that amountIn > 0. With amountIn = 0: amountOut = 0, the reserve sufficiency check passes trivially, transferFrom(msg.sender, this, 0) and transfer(msg.sender, 0) are called. This emits a FeeSwap event with zero amounts, which could confuse off-chain systems that track swap volumes or trigger downstream logic. Additionally, for tokens that revert on zero transfer, this creates a DoS vector for event-based monitoring. More critically, this allows interacting with pools without economic cost, which could be leveraged in combination with callback tokens for reentrancy setups.
threats.investigation_rationale: Line ~110 computes amountOut = (amountIn * M) / SCALE without checking amountIn != 0. Unlike rebalanceSwap which explicitly checks amountOut != 0, executeFeeSwap has no such guard.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No require(amountIn > 0) check; amountOut = (0 * M) / SCALE = 0; proceeds to call transferFrom and transfer with 0

preconditions: Any pool exists
Caller can call executeFeeSwap with amountIn = 0

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Zero-value transfers and misleading events; potential reentrancy amplifier via zero-cost token interactions

analysis_questions: Can zero-amount executeFeeSwap calls be chained with callback tokens for reentrancy at zero cost?
Do off-chain systems rely on FeeSwap events for accounting/monitoring?
Does zero-value transferFrom trigger callbacks on ERC-777 tokens?
validation_requirements: Call executeFeeSwap with amountIn = 0, verify it succeeds
Check if zero-value transferFrom triggers ERC-777 hooks
Assess off-chain event processing impact

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker calls executeFeeSwap(userToken, validatorToken, 0) repeatedly. Each call emits FeeSwap events with zero amounts, polluting event logs. If userToken has callback hooks, the zero-value transferFrom triggers the hook, enabling reentrancy setup without economic cost.
prerequisites: No gas cost concern for attacker

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Limited direct financial impact; primarily enables griefing and reentrancy setups
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Off-chain systems processing events; potential indirect impact on all pool users via reentrancy
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Events are permanent; reentrancy damage may be irreversible

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for zero-amount swap edge case

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable via event monitoring for zero-amount swaps

threats.title: LP Virtual Price Decrease via executeFeeSwap Rounding Enables Slow Value Extraction from LPs
threats.hypothesis_description: The LP virtual price is defined as (reserveValidatorToken + N * reserveUserToken / SCALE) / totalSupply. executeFeeSwap converts userToken to validatorToken at rate M/SCALE (0.997), increasing reserveUserToken and decreasing reserveValidatorToken. The effective value change per unit swapped is: +1 userToken (valued at N/SCALE ≈ 0.9985 in the LP formula) minus 1 * M/SCALE = 0.997 validatorToken. Net change = +0.9985 - 0.997 = +0.0015 per unit, which is positive. However, the integer truncation in amountOut = (amountIn * M) / SCALE can cause micro-losses. For very small amountIn values, truncation can make the effective fee larger than intended, but this is favorable to LPs. Conversely, for specific amounts where N * reserveUserToken / SCALE truncation compounds, the virtual price could decrease slightly across many small operations.
threats.investigation_rationale: The economic invariant INV-ECON-001 requires virtual price monotonicity. Integer truncation in the fixed-rate formula (amountOut = amountIn * 9970 / 10000) and in the LP value formula (N * reserveUserToken / SCALE) may compound to violate this invariant under specific amount patterns.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Integer division truncation in amountOut = (amountIn * M) / SCALE and in denom = reserveValidatorToken + (N * reserveUserToken) / SCALE

preconditions: Multiple small swaps executed to accumulate truncation errors
Specific amountIn values chosen to maximize truncation impact
LP virtual price tracked across operations

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Integer division truncation in fee calculation and LP value formula
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Compound truncation across executeFeeSwap and mint LP calculation may allow virtual price to decrease
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No virtual price invariant check after state updates

analysis_questions: Does the virtual price strictly increase for every executeFeeSwap, or can truncation cause decrease?
What specific amountIn values maximize truncation-induced LP value loss?
Is the cumulative effect significant enough to be exploitable for profit?
validation_requirements: Mathematically prove or disprove virtual price monotonicity for all valid inputs
Fuzz test with many small swaps and track virtual price changes
Calculate maximum extractable value from truncation compound errors

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker performs many small executeFeeSwaps with carefully chosen amounts that maximize truncation losses. Each swap slightly decreases the virtual price. Attacker then provides liquidity at the reduced virtual price, effectively buying LP shares cheap. Subsequent swaps or rebalances restore the virtual price, and attacker burns LP tokens at higher value.
prerequisites: Precise amount calculation to exploit truncation

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Marginal per-swap LP value loss; cumulative effect potentially significant for high-volume pools
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Fees from future swaps may offset losses if attack is small-scale

threats.test_coverage_gap: No fuzz tests for virtual price monotonicity across swap sequences

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Many small transactions required; gas cost may exceed profit
technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Minimal per-swap, but many swaps needed
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Trackable by monitoring LP virtual price changes per swap

threats.title: Unchecked liquidity view can be abused to revert integrator transactions (division/multiplication edge) and bypass intended pre-checks
threats.hypothesis_description: checkSufficientLiquidity() computes amountOutNeeded = (maxAmount * M) / SCALE without guarding against maxAmount overflow in intermediate multiplication (though Solidity 0.8 reverts) and without validating pool existence/token identity. If an integrating contract relies on this view for gating (e.g., via staticcall that must succeed), an attacker can supply extreme maxAmount to force a revert and block the integrator’s higher-level operation.
threats.investigation_rationale: While view functions alone are typically not security issues, this one is explicitly described as a pre-check endpoint and could be called by other contracts to gate swaps/withdrawals. If it is used on-chain, attacker-controlled inputs could cause deterministic reverts (panic on overflow) and DoS an integrator workflow.
deployment_assumptions: A downstream on-chain integrator contract calls FeeAMM.checkSufficientLiquidity() inside a state-changing path and treats revert as failure of the whole operation
Integrator forwards untrusted user-controlled maxAmount into the call
threats.config_evidence: No deployment/integration contracts provided; must confirm whether any on-chain consumer calls checkSufficientLiquidity() as a gating step.

proof_of_signal.pattern: amountOutNeeded = (maxAmount * M) / SCALE; no maxAmount bounds; potential overflow revert in intermediate multiply

preconditions: An external contract calls checkSufficientLiquidity() within a state-changing function
Attacker can influence maxAmount passed to that call
Integrator does not cap maxAmount and does not handle revert gracefully

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: unchecked arithmetic in a view used as a guard (potential overflow revert)
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: attacker-provided maxAmount can force revert, blocking the integrator flow
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No input bounds / no try-catch patterns possible inside FeeAMM

analysis_questions: Is checkSufficientLiquidity() called by any state-changing function in this repo or known integrations?
Do integrators bound maxAmount or derive it from trusted values?
Can overflow be triggered with realistic calldata sizes/values used by integrators?
validation_requirements: Search integration code for on-chain calls to checkSufficientLiquidity()
Property test with fuzzed maxAmount to confirm revert conditions
If used on-chain, add caps or redesign to avoid overflow-prone multiply-first arithmetic

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker calls an integrator entry point with maxAmount = type(uint256).max. Integrator calls FeeAMM.checkSufficientLiquidity(); multiplication overflows and reverts, causing integrator operation to fail repeatedly (DoS).
prerequisites: Presence of an on-chain integrator using checkSufficientLiquidity() as a guard

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Operational DoS; could block swaps/settlements routed through an integrator
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires integrator fix or input caps; FeeAMM itself cannot mitigate post-deploy

threats.test_coverage_gap: No evidence of fuzz tests around checkSufficientLiquidity maxAmount bounds/overflow behavior

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: reverts are observable; track repeated failures in downstream contract if present

threats.title: burn() Reentrancy via Token Transfer Hooks Enables Complete Pool Drain
threats.hypothesis_description: burn() executes both IERC20(userToken).transfer and IERC20(validatorToken).transfer BEFORE updating liquidityBalances, totalSupply, or pool reserves. If either token implements transfer hooks (ERC-777 tokensReceived, ERC-1363, or any callback-on-receive token), the recipient contract can reenter burn() with the same unchanged liquidityBalances, burning the same LP tokens repeatedly to extract multiples of their fair share. Pool creation is permissionless with no token whitelist, so an attacker can deploy a hook-enabled token, create a pool, and exploit once other LPs deposit.
threats.investigation_rationale: The checks-effects-interactions pattern is violated in the most dangerous way possible: TWO external calls before ANY state update. Each reentrant call sees identical pre-burn state, enabling N-fold extraction where N is limited only by gas/stack depth.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, amountUserToken) and IERC20(validatorToken).transfer(to, amountValidatorToken) execute BEFORE liquidityBalances[poolId][msg.sender] -= liquidity, totalSupply[poolId] -= liquidity, pool.reserveUserToken -= uint128(amountUserToken), pool.reserveValidatorToken -= uint128(amountValidatorToken)

preconditions: Pool uses at least one token with transfer hooks (ERC-777, ERC-1363, or custom callback)
Attacker holds LP tokens (liquidityBalances > 0) in the target pool
Pool has reserves from other LPs to drain beyond attacker's fair share
Sufficient gas for reentrant call depth

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: amountUserToken = (liquidity * pool.reserveUserToken) / _totalSupply computed once, but extractable N times before state update
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Each reentrant burn() call sees unchanged liquidityBalances[msg.sender], totalSupply, and pool reserves, extracting the same proportional amount each time
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Profit = (N - 1) × (liquidity / totalSupply) × totalPoolValue, where N = number of reentrant calls

economic_questions: What is the maximum reentrant depth before gas exhaustion on the target L2?
Are any TIP-20 stablecoins ERC-777 compatible or have transfer hooks?
What is the total TVL at risk across all pools?
validation_requirements: Deploy proof-of-concept with ERC-777 token and verify multi-burn extraction
Measure gas cost per reentrant level on target chain
Verify Solidity 0.8 checked arithmetic on final unwinding doesn't prevent outer calls from completing

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal initial LP deposit (e.g., 1000 tokens) plus ERC-777 token deployment (~0.01 ETH)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for reentrant calls, estimated 500K-2M gas on L2 (~$0.10-$1.00)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 100% of pool reserves beyond attacker's own deposit
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: >10000% ROI if pool has significant other LP deposits

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: No external liquidity needed; exploit is internal to the pool
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After significant LP deposits accumulate in the target pool
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Pool is completely drained; all other LPs lose their entire deposit

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All other LPs in the pool
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire pool TVL minus attacker's own deposit

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: No reentrancy guard, no CEI compliance, state updates at lines 212-215 after transfers at lines 207-208
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify that uint128 underflow on reserve subtraction during unwinding doesn't revert outer calls if attacker sizes withdrawals correctly

threats.title: rebalanceSwap() Optimistic State Update Enables Phantom Reserve Exploitation via Cross-Function Reentrancy
threats.hypothesis_description: rebalanceSwap() updates pool.reserveValidatorToken += amountIn and pool.reserveUserToken -= amountOut BEFORE calling IERC20(validatorToken).transferFrom. If validatorToken has hooks, the attacker's callback fires after reserves are optimistically inflated but before tokens actually arrive. The attacker reenters burn() during this callback: the inflated reserveValidatorToken causes _calculateBurnAmounts to return more validatorToken than actually exists in the contract. The attacker extracts phantom reserves, making the pool insolvent.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is uniquely dangerous because state is updated BEFORE the external call (optimistic update), meaning the reentered function sees INFLATED reserves, not stale ones. burn() proportionally distributes based on these inflated reserves, transferring more validatorToken than the contract actually holds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: pool.reserveValidatorToken += uint128(amountIn) at line 150 and pool.reserveUserToken -= uint128(amountOut) at line 151 execute BEFORE IERC20(validatorToken).transferFrom at line 153; reserves reflect tokens not yet received

preconditions: validatorToken has transfer hooks (ERC-777 tokensToSend on sender side during transferFrom)
Attacker holds LP tokens in the target pool
Pool has sufficient reserves for the burn to extract meaningful value
Attacker can trigger callback during transferFrom

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: After optimistic update: reserveValidatorToken = original + amountIn (inflated). burn's _calculateBurnAmounts: amountValidatorToken = liquidity * inflatedReserveVT / totalSupply
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker chooses large amountIn in rebalanceSwap to maximally inflate reserveValidatorToken. During callback, burns LP tokens against inflated reserves. Contract transfers out validatorTokens it doesn't actually have.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Profit ≈ (amountIn / totalSupply) × liquidity_held, extracted from other LPs' deposits

economic_questions: Can the attacker's transferFrom hook fire before actual token transfer occurs?
What is the maximum inflation achievable without causing overflow?
Does the final validatorToken.transfer in burn() fail due to insufficient actual balance?
validation_requirements: Build ERC-777 validatorToken and test: rebalanceSwap inflates reserves → callback → burn extracts based on inflated amount
Verify that IERC20(validatorToken).transfer in burn doesn't revert due to actual balance being less than calculated amount
Calculate maximum extractable phantom reserves

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: LP tokens + enough validatorToken allowance for rebalanceSwap (tokens never actually leave attacker if callback aborts transfer)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Single transaction gas cost on L2 (~$0.50-$2.00)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Proportional to inflation factor × attacker's LP share
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Potentially >1000% of attacker's original LP investment

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When pool has accumulated significant reserves from other LPs
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Pool becomes insolvent; remaining LPs cannot withdraw full value

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All other LPs in the pool
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Partial to complete loss depending on inflation amount
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All validatorToken reserves in excess of actual contract balance

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Overflow check at line 146 limits inflation: uint256(pool.reserveValidatorToken) + amountIn <= type(uint128).max
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify burn's IERC20(validatorToken).transfer will revert when actual balance < calculated amountValidatorToken from inflated reserves

threats.title: Missing Decimal Normalization Allows Complete Pool Draining
threats.hypothesis_description: The AMM hardcodes a 1:1 exchange rate (minus fees) using constants M and N, but fails to normalize token decimals. If a pool is created with tokens of differing decimals, the math breaks catastrophically, allowing an attacker to drain the contract's entire balance of the higher-decimal token for pennies.
threats.investigation_rationale: DeFi protocols must account for varying token decimals (e.g., USDC has 6, DAI has 18). Applying a fixed multiplier to raw token amounts without decimal scaling creates massive arbitrage opportunities.
deployment_assumptions: Pool is created with stablecoins having different decimals (e.g., USDC and DAI)
threats.config_evidence: No decimal normalization logic exists in FeeAMM.sol

proof_of_signal.pattern: amountOut = (amountIn * M) / SCALE; (direct math on raw amounts)

preconditions: A pool exists where userToken and validatorToken have different decimals
The contract holds a balance of the higher-decimal token (from this or other pools)

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Inputting 1 unit of a 18-decimal token yields ~1 unit of a 6-decimal token, but inputting 1 unit of a 6-decimal token yields ~1 unit of an 18-decimal token (a 1,000,000,000,000x overpayment)
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker swaps 1 wei of a 6-decimal token to extract 0.997 * 10^18 wei of the 18-decimal token, draining the pool instantly.

economic_questions: Which stablecoins are intended to be supported?
Do any intended tokens have differing decimals?
validation_requirements: Deploy a test pool with 6-decimal and 18-decimal tokens
Execute a swap and verify the massive imbalance in transferred value

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Entire balance of the higher-decimal token held by the contract

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Contract must hold liquidity of the target token
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time after liquidity is deposited
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Total loss of funds for LPs of the affected token

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All reserves of the higher-decimal token across all pools

threats.title: Fixed-Rate Pricing Model Vulnerable to Stablecoin Depeg Arbitrage
threats.hypothesis_description: The AMM relies on a hardcoded 1:1 pricing model (M=9970, N=9985) without any price oracle, bonding curve, or slippage adjustment. If a supported stablecoin depegs, arbitrageurs will drain the pool of the healthy token, leaving LPs with only the depegged asset.
threats.investigation_rationale: Stablecoins frequently experience temporary or permanent depegs (e.g., USDC in 2023, UST). A fixed-rate AMM acts as a naive limit order, absorbing 100% of the impermanent loss when market prices diverge from the hardcoded rate.

proof_of_signal.pattern: amountOut = (amountIn * M) / SCALE; (fixed rate regardless of market price)

preconditions: One of the pooled stablecoins depegs from $1.00 by more than the 0.3% fee margin
Sufficient liquidity exists in the pool

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Price is always exactly 0.997 or 0.9985, regardless of external market value
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Arbitrageurs buy the depegged token cheaply on external markets and swap it for the healthy token at the fixed 1:1 rate in the AMM.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Profit = (Value of healthy token extracted) - (Cost of depegged token inputted) - (0.3% fee)

economic_questions: What is the historical volatility of the supported stablecoins?
Is there any off-chain mechanism to pause the contract during a depeg?
validation_requirements: Model LP losses during a simulated 5% depeg event
Calculate the exact arbitrage threshold based on the M and N constants

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Depends on pool size; flash loans can be used
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Proportional to the depeg percentage and pool size
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Continuous as long as depeg persists

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: External market liquidity for the depegged token
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: LPs suffer severe impermanent loss, effectively holding the bag for the depegged asset

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss proportional to the depeg severity
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Total value of the healthy token in the pool

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: M and N fee margins vs historical stablecoin volatility

threats.title: rebalanceSwap Optimistic State Update + Unchecked transferFrom Return Value Enables Free Token Extraction
threats.hypothesis_description: rebalanceSwap updates reserves (reserveValidatorToken += amountIn, reserveUserToken -= amountOut) BEFORE calling IERC20(validatorToken).transferFrom. The return value of transferFrom is not checked (no SafeERC20). If validatorToken returns false instead of reverting on failure, the state updates persist but no tokens are received. The subsequent IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, amountOut) sends real userTokens to the attacker for free. The attacker simply calls rebalanceSwap with zero allowance; transferFrom returns false; they receive userTokens without paying.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is uniquely dangerous because the state-before-transfer pattern in rebalanceSwap means ANY token that returns false on transferFrom failure (rather than reverting) creates a direct extraction vulnerability. The contract uses bare IERC20 interface without SafeERC20, so return values are discarded.

proof_of_signal.pattern: pool.reserveValidatorToken += uint128(amountIn) and pool.reserveUserToken -= uint128(amountOut) at ~lines 148-149; IERC20(validatorToken).transferFrom at ~line 151 (return value unchecked); IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, amountOut) at ~line 155

preconditions: validatorToken returns false (not reverts) on failed transferFrom
Pool has non-zero reserveUserToken
Attacker has zero allowance or zero balance for validatorToken
userToken.transfer succeeds (standard reverting behavior)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: State updates at lines ~148-149 before transferFrom at ~151. IERC20 interface returns bool but code does not check it. Discarded return value enables silent failures.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No SafeERC20 library used anywhere in contract
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent — any call to rebalanceSwap with a non-reverting-on-fail validatorToken

operational_questions: Which TIP-20 stablecoin implementations return false vs revert on transfer failure?
Are there any pools where validatorToken has non-reverting failure behavior?
Can a non-reverting token be introduced via permissionless pool creation?
validation_requirements: Test with ERC20 that returns false on insufficient allowance
Verify that rebalanceSwap proceeds to transfer userTokens after silent transferFrom failure
Quantify maximum extractable amount per call

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify or create pool where validatorToken returns false on transferFrom failure
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call rebalanceSwap with zero allowance for validatorToken and desired amountOut
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: transferFrom returns false silently; state already updated; userToken transferred to attacker

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete drain of userToken reserves from affected pool
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Pool becomes insolvent; reserves show phantom validatorToken balances
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All LPs and swappers in the affected pool

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Low — no custom contract needed, just call with zero allowance

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: RebalanceSwap events where sender has zero allowance for validatorToken
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: reserveValidatorToken growing without corresponding balance increase

threats.title: No Emergency Pause or Recovery Mechanism Prevents Halting Active Exploits
threats.hypothesis_description: FeeAMM has no owner, no admin, no Pausable inheritance, no emergency stop mechanism, and no upgradeability. If any of the reentrancy or accounting vulnerabilities (OPS-H001 through OPS-H008) are actively exploited, there is NO on-chain mechanism to halt the attack, freeze affected pools, or recover stolen funds. The contract is permanently immutable and uncontrollable once deployed.
threats.investigation_rationale: For a contract holding financial assets with multiple critical code-level vulnerabilities, the complete absence of ANY emergency control mechanism elevates operational risk. While immutability is sometimes desirable, combined with the identified reentrancy and accounting bugs, it means any exploit results in total, irreversible loss.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No Ownable/Pausable/AccessControl import or inheritance. No admin address variable. No whenNotPaused modifier. No pause()/unpause() functions. No proxy pattern. Contract is fully immutable and uncontrollable.

preconditions: An exploitable vulnerability exists (established by OPS-H001 through OPS-H008)
Contract is deployed and holds value

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Complete absence of emergency controls in a contract with multiple identified vulnerabilities
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin exists — this IS the finding

operational_questions: Can the protocol's frontend/UI be paused to prevent user interaction during an exploit?
Are there off-chain monitoring systems that can detect and alert on reentrancy patterns?
Can individual pool tokens be paused at the token contract level?
validation_requirements: Confirm no hidden admin or upgrade mechanism exists
Assess whether token-level pausing could serve as an emergency control

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: N/A — this is a missing defense, not an attack
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Impact amplification: any successful exploit (OPS-H001-H008) cannot be stopped
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker can repeatedly exploit across multiple pools without risk of contract-level intervention
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All pools drained before off-chain responses can be coordinated

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Exploits run to completion without any circuit breaker
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Impossible on-chain; requires contract redeployment and user migration
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All pools and all users across the entire contract

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: N/A — no monitoring hooks in contract
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Large unexpected outflows detectable only via external monitoring

threats.title: Incompatible IERC20 Interface Causes Permanent DoS for Non-Standard Stablecoins
threats.hypothesis_description: The contract uses the standard IERC20 interface which expects a boolean return value. Non-standard stablecoins like USDT do not return a boolean on transfer/transferFrom. The EVM will revert these calls, rendering the AMM completely unusable for the most popular USD stablecoin.
threats.investigation_rationale: Since the protocol is explicitly designed for 'USD stablecoins', the inability to support USDT represents a critical failure of the core business logic and prevents protocol adoption.
deployment_assumptions: Pool is created using USDT or other non-standard ERC20 tokens that do not return a boolean
threats.config_evidence: FeeAMM.sol imports standard OpenZeppelin IERC20.sol and does not use SafeERC20.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(userToken).transferFrom(msg.sender, address(this), amountIn) without SafeERC20

preconditions: Target token does not return a boolean on transfer/transferFrom (e.g., USDT on mainnet)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Standard interface expects 32 bytes of return data, EVM reverts if 0 bytes returned
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Any interaction with the pool will revert, locking any accidentally sent funds or preventing usage
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit, but causes complete denial of service and potential loss of utility

economic_questions: Which specific target chains and stablecoins are intended for deployment?
Are there any wrapper contracts planned to handle non-standard tokens?
validation_requirements: Test contract interactions using a mainnet fork with actual USDT contract
Verify EVM behavior on the specific target chain

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Prevents market making for major stablecoins

threats.title: Missing Slippage Protection Exposes LPs to Severe Composition Risk
threats.hypothesis_description: The burn function lacks minimum output parameters. If the pool's composition changes significantly between transaction submission and execution, the LP will receive an unexpected and potentially unfavorable ratio of tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: LPs expect a certain ratio of tokens when withdrawing. Without slippage bounds, normal market activity (like a large rebalanceSwap) can drastically alter the payout, causing economic harm to the LP.
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence required; inherent to contract logic.

proof_of_signal.pattern: burn signature lacks minAmountUserToken and minAmountValidatorToken parameters

preconditions: LP submits a burn transaction
Another user executes a large swap before the burn executes

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: reserveUserToken and reserveValidatorToken can be heavily skewed by preceding transactions
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attackers can intentionally skew the pool to force LPs to absorb unwanted inventory

economic_questions: What is the maximum composition shift possible in a single block?
How much value can an LP lose due to receiving the less desirable token?
validation_requirements: Simulate a large rebalanceSwap immediately followed by a burn
Calculate the value difference of the received tokens vs expected tokens

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Sufficient capital to significantly move pool reserves
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends on the external market value difference between the two stablecoins
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Low, primarily causes loss to victim rather than direct profit

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Large liquidity relative to pool size
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When an LP submits a large burn transaction
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Forces LPs to bear inventory risk without consent

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Receiving 100% of one token instead of a 50/50 split
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: The LP's entire withdrawn liquidity is subject to composition risk

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Pool reserve ratios before and after large swaps
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Value of withdrawn tokens under extreme pool skews

threats.title: Cross-Pool Token Balance Drainage via Reentrancy Amplification Through Shared Token Balances
threats.hypothesis_description: getPoolId creates separate pool IDs for different token pairs, but the contract holds all tokens in a single address with no global balance accounting. When an attacker exploits burn reentrancy in a pool containing a callback-enabled token (ERC-777) paired with a legitimate stablecoin (e.g., USDT), the drained USDT comes from the contract's global balance—not a pool-specific sub-account. This means USDT reserved for OTHER pools (e.g., Pool(USDC, USDT)) is also stolen, making unrelated LP positions insolvent.
threats.investigation_rationale: The basic burn reentrancy (INV-STATE-001) drains one pool, but with multiple pools sharing the same token, the blast radius extends to all pools using that token. getPoolId's design allows an attacker to create a purpose-built malicious pool to target tokens held across all pools.
deployment_assumptions: At least one callback-enabled token (ERC-777/ERC-1363) can be used as userToken or validatorToken
Multiple pools share the same token as validatorToken
threats.config_evidence: No deployment scripts provided showing token whitelist or restricted pool creation. FeeAMM.sol mint() at lines 160-203 has no token validation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: getPoolId creates isolated pool accounting via keccak256(abi.encode(userToken, validatorToken)) but the actual ERC-20 balance is global; burn() performs IERC20.transfer BEFORE updating pool.reserve*, allowing reentrancy that drains the shared global balance

preconditions: Attacker can create a pool with a callback-enabled token (ERC-777) as userToken or validatorToken
At least one other legitimate pool exists using the same token as validatorToken
No external token whitelist or pool creation restrictions exist

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: burn() transfers tokens at lines 225-226 BEFORE updating state at lines 228-231; getPoolId at line 79 allows any token addresses
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Create Pool(erc777Token, USDT), provide liquidity, call burn. During erc777Token.transfer callback, reenter burn repeatedly. Each reentry drains USDT from global contract balance, not just the malicious pool's allocation.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: attacker_profit = (num_reentries * proportional_USDT_per_burn) - initial_LP_deposit. With sufficient reentries, total USDT across ALL pools can be drained.

economic_questions: What is the total USDT TVL across all pools that share the same validatorToken?
How many reentrant calls can fit within block gas limits?
Can the attacker create a pool with ERC-777 token at minimal cost?
validation_requirements: Verify ERC-777 tokensReceived callback triggers during burn's transfer
Confirm that reentering burn with unchanged liquidityBalances extracts tokens each time
Quantify total extractable value across all pools sharing the drained token

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal: cost of deploying ERC-777 token + initial LP deposit (recoverable via the exploit itself)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: ~0.05 ETH in gas for deployment + reentrant burn calls
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 100% of all contract-held tokens of the targeted type across ALL pools

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: None; attacker provides own ERC-777 token
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When total TVL in the targeted token is highest
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Catastrophic: complete insolvency for all pools using the drained token

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All LPs in any pool using the same token as the drained one
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Up to 100% loss of their share of the drained token
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All tokens of the targeted type held by the contract

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Number of pools sharing a token, total reserves across those pools
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm reentrant burn extracts proportional share each time without state update

threats.title: checkSufficientLiquidity Reports False Positive Under Cross-Pool Balance Competition
threats.hypothesis_description: checkSufficientLiquidity validates against the pool's internal reserveValidatorToken accounting, not the contract's actual ERC-20 balance. When multiple pools share the same validatorToken, the aggregate reserves can equal the actual balance. If any pool's accounting becomes desynchronized (via fee-on-transfer tokens, unchecked transfer return values, or reentrancy in another pool), checkSufficientLiquidity reports sufficient liquidity while the actual balance is insufficient. External protocols relying on this check to pre-validate swaps will encounter unexpected reverts during executeFeeSwap.
threats.investigation_rationale: checkSufficientLiquidity is the only pre-flight check function. External integrators (payment routers, multi-hop swap aggregators) likely call it before committing to an executeFeeSwap path. A false positive causes cascading failures in multi-step transactions that have already committed other resources.
deployment_assumptions: Multiple pools share the same validatorToken
At least one pool has experienced accounting desync (e.g., fee-on-transfer token in another pool)
threats.config_evidence: No token validation in mint(). checkSufficientLiquidity at FeeAMM.sol:89-96 checks only pools[poolId].reserveValidatorToken.

proof_of_signal.pattern: checkSufficientLiquidity checks pools[poolId].reserveValidatorToken (internal accounting) without comparing to IERC20(validatorToken).balanceOf(address(this)); executeFeeSwap trusts same internal accounting

preconditions: Multiple pools use the same validatorToken
At least one pool's internal accounting exceeds actual token balance (from fee-on-transfer, unchecked return, or reentrancy)
External protocol relies on checkSufficientLiquidity as a pre-validation gate

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: checkSufficientLiquidity: amountOutNeeded = (maxAmount * M) / SCALE; check is against internal reserves only
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Desynchronize one pool's accounting (e.g., via fee-on-transfer token), then checkSufficientLiquidity passes for a different pool but actual transfer fails
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Indirect: causes DoS/failure for integrating protocols, potential griefing of multi-step transactions

economic_questions: Are there external protocols that rely on checkSufficientLiquidity to route swaps?
What is the cost to an integrating protocol when a pre-validated swap reverts?
How easily can accounting desync be triggered in a neighboring pool?
validation_requirements: Create two pools sharing validatorToken, desync one via fee-on-transfer, verify checkSufficientLiquidity still passes for the other
Confirm executeFeeSwap reverts with actual balance shortfall despite checkSufficientLiquidity passing

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal: just need to trigger accounting desync in one pool
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect: griefing impact on integrating protocols

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After accounting desync in any pool sharing the token

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Failed transactions, wasted gas, potential fund locking in multi-step operations
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Depends on integrating protocol's transaction value

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare internal reserves vs actual balances under various desync scenarios

threats.title: Missing Cumulative Reserve Overflow Check in mint() and executeFeeSwap() Unlike rebalanceSwap()
threats.hypothesis_description: _requireU128 validates that individual amounts fit in uint128, but mint() and executeFeeSwap() lack the explicit cumulative overflow check that rebalanceSwap() has at lines 134-136: `if (uint256(pool.reserveValidatorToken) + amountIn > type(uint128).max) revert InsufficientReserves()`. In executeFeeSwap, `pool.reserveUserToken += uint128(amountIn)` can overflow uint128 if current reserve + amountIn exceeds the max, causing a Solidity Panic(0x11) instead of a descriptive InsufficientReserves error. In mint, `pool.reserveValidatorToken += uint128(amountValidatorToken)` has the same gap. This inconsistency is a demonstrable code defect: the developer clearly intended cumulative overflow checks (evidenced by rebalanceSwap) but omitted them in two other functions.
threats.investigation_rationale: While Solidity 0.8 checked arithmetic prevents silent overflow, the Panic revert is unrecoverable and provides no useful error information. More critically, if a future optimizer or compiler change affects checked arithmetic behavior, or if the contract is used as a library target for delegatecall from unchecked contexts, the missing guard becomes exploitable. The inconsistency also suggests the developer may have missed other checks.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _requireU128 checks individual amount fits uint128; rebalanceSwap has cumulative check at line 134 `if (uint256(pool.reserveValidatorToken) + amountIn > type(uint128).max)`; executeFeeSwap line 110 `pool.reserveUserToken += uint128(amountIn)` and mint line 199 `pool.reserveValidatorToken += uint128(amountValidatorToken)` both lack equivalent cumulative checks

preconditions: Reserve of the target token type is already close to uint128 max (~3.4e38)
A deposit or swap pushes the cumulative reserve past uint128 max

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: rebalanceSwap: explicit check `uint256(pool.reserveValidatorToken) + amountIn > type(uint128).max` at line 134. executeFeeSwap and mint: no equivalent check before `+= uint128(amount)`
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Code inconsistency: identical operation handled differently across functions. If conditions ever allow near-max reserves, executeFeeSwap and mint produce opaque Panic reverts while rebalanceSwap gives descriptive errors.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Primarily a code correctness defect; could enable DoS by pushing reserves to near-max through repeated deposits in low-decimal tokens

economic_questions: Can reserves practically approach uint128 max for any supported token?
Does the inconsistency create differential behavior exploitable by integrating contracts?
Are there tokens with sufficiently low decimals to make near-max reserves feasible?
validation_requirements: Confirm Panic revert occurs in executeFeeSwap/mint when cumulative exceeds uint128 max
Verify rebalanceSwap correctly handles the same condition with InsufficientReserves
Test with low-decimal tokens (0-2 decimals) to assess practical reachability

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Impractical for 18-decimal tokens; potentially feasible for 0-2 decimal tokens
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Multiple large deposits to push reserves near max
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: DoS value; no direct profit extraction
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: N/A - code defect, not direct profit exploit

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Opaque reverts could confuse integrating protocols

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users and integrating protocols encountering unexpected Panic reverts

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: uint128 max = 3.4e38; for 6-decimal tokens = 3.4e32 tokens; for 18-decimal tokens = 3.4e20 tokens
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Side-by-side comparison of rebalanceSwap line 134-136 vs executeFeeSwap line 110 vs mint line 199

threats.title: Accounting Desynchronization via Rebasing Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: The AMM relies entirely on internal reserve accounting rather than checking actual token balances. If a rebasing token is used, positive rebases will permanently lock the excess tokens in the contract, while negative rebases will cause the actual balance to drop below the recorded reserves, bricking pool operations.
threats.investigation_rationale: Strict internal accounting without balance reconciliation is incompatible with elastic supply tokens.
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence required; vulnerability exists in core logic.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Internal reserve accounting (reserveUserToken, reserveValidatorToken) used instead of actual token balances

preconditions: Pool is created using an elastic supply (rebasing) token

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No mechanism exists to sync internal reserves with actual contract balances

operational_questions: Are rebasing tokens supported or expected?
Can an attacker force a rebase to intentionally brick a pool?
validation_requirements: Deploy pool with a rebasing token
Trigger a negative rebase and verify that burn() reverts

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for or trigger a negative rebase event
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Actual contract balance drops below internal reserves
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Subsequent swaps or burns revert when attempting to transfer out tokens the contract no longer holds

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Denial of service for pool withdrawals and swaps

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Mismatch between reserves and actual balances

threats.title: Permissionless poolId spoofing via token-pair ordering mismatch can divert swaps/liquidity into the wrong pool (funds effectively stuck/lost)
threats.hypothesis_description: Pool identity uses keccak256(abi.encode(userToken, validatorToken)) with no canonical ordering and no validation in read helper/UX flows. If integrators/validators compute poolId with a different convention (sorted addresses) or accidentally reverse arguments, they may mint/swap against a different pool than intended. An attacker can exploit this operationally by seeding a “shadow” pool for the reversed pair, then inducing routing to the attacker-seeded pool, causing users to receive unexpected token flows and potentially trapping value in illiquid/incorrect pools.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a realistic operational failure mode because the contract allows arbitrary pool creation and has no canonicalization. In production, routers/indexers/validators often normalize token pairs; a mismatch can be exploited without mempool visibility and without privileged access.
deployment_assumptions: At least one on-chain/off-chain integration (router, validator fee collector, UI, indexer) derives pool identifiers using a different convention than FeeAMM.getPoolId(userToken, validatorToken), e.g., sorted token addresses or reversed parameters
Users/validators follow that integration to choose pool addresses/arguments
threats.config_evidence: No router/integration code provided; risk depends on external operational components using inconsistent poolId derivation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: getPoolId(userToken,validatorToken)=keccak256(abi.encode(userToken,validatorToken)) with no canonical ordering; no global registry/validation of pairs

preconditions: There exists at least one intended token pair (A,B) used operationally
Operational tooling sometimes passes (B,A) or sorts addresses when it should not
Attackers can create/seeding liquidity in the unintended direction-pool (B,A) to make it appear usable

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Different components may treat the pair as unordered, while FeeAMM treats it as ordered; shadow pools can exist for both (A,B) and (B,A)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Persistent once any integration mismatch exists

operational_questions: Do validators/Tempo payment flows always call FeeAMM with the same (userToken, validatorToken) ordering as used for mint/burn accounting?
Do any indexers/routers normalize/sort token addresses for pool lookup?
Are there monitoring/alerts for duplicate pools on reversed pairs?
validation_requirements: Review operational code that selects pool arguments and calls FeeAMM
Scan chain state for both (A,B) and (B,A) pools being used for the same intended pair
Simulate integration calls with swapped arguments and quantify user impact

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Create a shadow pool for reversed pair (B,A) via minting minimal liquidity (if any LP tooling will route to it)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Rely on integration mismatch to route swaps/mints to the shadow pool
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Exploit user confusion/illiquidity: users may be unable to exit due to poor reserves or may receive wrong token outputs
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Optionally cycle liquidity between pools to exacerbate availability failures
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Low on-chain coordination; main requirement is an observable integration mismatch

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: High: fee conversions and liquidity operations may go to unintended pools, causing settlement failures and user/validator accounting breaks
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium: may require operational migration, user education, and/or deploying a new AMM with canonical pair handling
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All pool interactions that depend on consistent pair identification (liquidity provisioning, fee swaps, rebalancing checks)

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Moderate (understanding of poolId derivation and integration behavior)
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Anytime; does not rely on transaction ordering

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Existence of duplicate pools for the same token pair in reverse order; liquidity distributed across both
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Failed fee conversion settlements; unexpected pool reserves in monitoring dashboards
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Swaps/mints occurring on a pool with reversed ordering relative to documented operational intent

threats.title: Liquidity availability checks can be bypassed or mislead integrators due to non-atomic checkSufficientLiquidity() vs swap execution (TOCTOU)
threats.hypothesis_description: Operational flows may call checkSufficientLiquidity() before executeFeeSwap() to decide if a fee conversion will succeed. Because the check is a separate transaction/call and uses a simplified formula (maxAmount * M / SCALE) against internal reserves, it can become stale immediately after any pool state change. Attackers can repeatedly perform on-chain swaps/rebalances between the check and the later swap execution (multi-block, no mempool needed), causing the operational system to attempt swaps that revert or to under-estimate failure rates, disrupting validator settlement operations.
threats.investigation_rationale: Phase A highlights operational reliance on deterministic fixed-rate swaps; a separate check function invites TOCTOU errors in production integrations, creating a realistic DoS vector against batch settlement systems that pre-check liquidity and then execute later.
deployment_assumptions: An operational component uses checkSufficientLiquidity() as a gating condition before submitting executeFeeSwap() in a later transaction/block
Operational component does not handle reverts gracefully (e.g., batch processing halts, retries cause cascading delays)
threats.config_evidence: No operational settlement/validator code provided; risk depends on how checkSufficientLiquidity() is used off-chain/on-chain as a preflight gate.

proof_of_signal.pattern: checkSufficientLiquidity computes amountOutNeeded and compares to reserves, but does not reserve liquidity or provide atomic execution with swaps

preconditions: Integrators/validators use checkSufficientLiquidity() to pre-validate swaps
There is any third-party activity changing reserves between pre-check and swap execution
Operational tooling treats pre-check success as strong guarantee

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Separate preflight check for liquidity without commitment/locking can become stale and cause systematic revert storms
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time pools are active; multi-block manipulation is sufficient

operational_questions: Is checkSufficientLiquidity() used in production as a required gate before executeFeeSwap()?
What is the retry/backoff behavior on swap failures in validator settlement flows?
Is there an atomic path that combines check + swap (e.g., via a router contract) or is it always separated?
validation_requirements: Review settlement system architecture for TOCTOU patterns
Run adversarial tests: repeatedly change reserves and measure failure rate of delayed swaps
Assess whether repeated reverts can halt batch processing (DoS amplification)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Observe target pool used by validator settlement system
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Between known settlement intervals, perform swaps/rebalances to reduce reserveValidatorToken below required output
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Cause settlement swaps to revert; repeat to sustain disruption
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Low; attacker only needs sufficient capital to temporarily deplete targeted reserves

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Medium: settlement delays, reverted batches, potential cascading failures in validator payment flows
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low to Medium: requires better retry logic or atomic execution paths
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Validator fee conversions using preflight liquidity checks

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any multi-block window; does not require mempool visibility

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Repeated reserve depletion events prior to settlement windows; spikes in reverted executeFeeSwap txs
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Batch job failures correlated with reserve movements
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Attacker address repeatedly interacting with the same pool without profit motive (pure disruption)

threats.title: uint128 reserve packing may cause unexpected revert/DoS when reserves grow, with inconsistent handling across functions
threats.hypothesis_description: Reserves are stored as uint128. Some paths explicitly check for overflow (rebalanceSwap checks reserveValidatorToken + amountIn), while mint and executeFeeSwap rely on implicit checked arithmetic when casting/adding to uint128. A pool approaching uint128.max can hit inconsistent revert reasons and become partially unusable (e.g., mint always reverting via panic) even while other operations might still be possible or expected to revert with domain-specific errors.
threats.investigation_rationale: While hitting uint128.max is unlikely for stablecoins, this is a correctness defect with operational impact: inconsistent overflow handling can break integrations, monitoring, and error recovery logic. If any tokens have very large decimals/supply or the system runs long enough, it can become a real DoS.
deployment_assumptions: A pool can accumulate reserves approaching type(uint128).max due to token decimals/supply or long-term operation
Integrations depend on stable error semantics (custom errors) rather than Panic codes
threats.config_evidence: FeeAMM.sol uses uint128 reserves and only rebalanceSwap includes an explicit overflow check; mint/executeFeeSwap do not.

proof_of_signal.pattern: reserves stored as uint128; helper only checks individual amounts, not reserve+amount accumulation

preconditions: Reserve values can grow close to uint128.max in a pool
Users attempt mint/executeFeeSwap/rebalanceSwap near the limit

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: amount bound check without reserve growth bound check
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: uint128 reserve additions may revert inconsistently and cause pool operation DoS
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing explicit reserve overflow checks in all reserve-increasing paths

analysis_questions: Are reserve additions in mint/executeFeeSwap protected by any implicit bounds elsewhere?
Should the protocol guarantee reserve magnitudes are far below uint128.max (token choice/decimals)?
Would panic-based reverts break any operational automation?
validation_requirements: Fuzz test reserve growth near uint128.max for all functions
Decide whether uint128 is necessary; consider uint256 or consistent overflow checks
Add explicit custom-error overflow checks for consistency if needed

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If a pool’s reserveValidatorToken is near uint128.max, an attacker can grief by inducing attempted mints that revert with Panic and disrupt automated systems expecting custom errors.
prerequisites: Extremely large reserves or unusual token configuration

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Mostly operational/availability impact; potential temporary lock of ability to add liquidity or swap
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: LPs and swap users of the affected pool
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: If near uint128.max, recovery may require migrating to a new pool/contract

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests indicated for boundary conditions around uint128 reserve limits

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: high only if attempting to reach uint128.max in production; low for simulation
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: panic reverts detectable via traces and error selectors

threats.title: Permissionless Pool Creation With Arbitrary Tokens Enables Social Engineering Fund Traps
threats.hypothesis_description: Pool creation via mint() has no token validation, no whitelist, and no governance approval. Anyone can create a pool pairing a worthless/malicious token with a legitimate stablecoin. Users or automated systems that interact with the pool (providing liquidity or swapping) based on token name/symbol alone may deposit real value into a trap. The attacker, as sole LP of the malicious side, can extract real tokens via rebalanceSwap or by manipulating the malicious token's behavior.
threats.investigation_rationale: The combination of permissionless pool creation, no token validation, and the reentrancy vulnerabilities (OPS-H001-005) creates a potent attack chain: create trap pool → attract deposits → exploit via reentrancy.

proof_of_signal.pattern: mint() only checks userToken != validatorToken. No validation that addresses are legitimate ERC20 contracts, no whitelist check, no governance gate.

preconditions: Users or protocols interact with pools without verifying token legitimacy
Attacker deploys token mimicking legitimate stablecoin name/symbol
Victims deposit real stablecoins as validatorToken into the trap pool

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Zero validation on token addresses in mint(). Any address accepted including EOAs, non-ERC20 contracts, or malicious tokens.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin to curate or remove malicious pools
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent — no governance to delist pools

operational_questions: Does the Tempo protocol UI/frontend filter pools by token whitelist?
Are validators or automated systems configured to interact with any pool?
Could pools with malicious tokens appear in DEX aggregator routing?
validation_requirements: Verify that pools with arbitrary addresses can be created and interacted with
Test if malicious token can be combined with reentrancy exploits

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deploy fake stablecoin with identical name/symbol/decimals as legitimate TIP-20 token
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Create pool via mint(fakeToken, realUSDC, amount, attacker) seeding with real USDC
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Pool appears legitimate to unsuspecting users/systems; attract LP deposits
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Execute reentrancy attack (OPS-H001/H002) to drain all real USDC from pool

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Trust in protocol's pool ecosystem undermined

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: New pools with unrecognized token addresses
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Pools with tokens sharing names of legitimate tokens but different addresses
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Pool creation followed by promotional activity then sudden drain

threats.title: Missing Identical Token Check in Swap Functions
threats.hypothesis_description: While `mint` and `burn` correctly verify that `userToken != validatorToken`, `executeFeeSwap` and `rebalanceSwap` lack this check. Although a pool with identical tokens cannot be initialized via `mint`, if an attacker finds a way to manipulate state or if future protocol upgrades allow direct reserve manipulation, swaps could be executed against a 'ghost pool' where `userToken == validatorToken`, leading to corrupted accounting.
threats.investigation_rationale: Inconsistent input validation across the contract surface. While currently mitigated by the `mint` restriction, it leaves a dormant attack vector if the pool state can be artificially created.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing if (userToken == validatorToken) revert IdenticalAddresses();

preconditions: Ability to create or fund a pool where userToken == validatorToken (currently blocked by mint, requiring a secondary exploit)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Swaps do not verify token distinctness

analysis_questions: Is there any edge case where a pool with identical tokens can be initialized?
What happens to the math if amountIn and amountOut refer to the same token balance?
validation_requirements: Attempt to force-initialize a ghost pool and execute a swap to observe state corruption

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1. Attacker finds a way to bypass the `mint` restriction (e.g., via a reentrancy state corruption). 2. Attacker calls `executeFeeSwap` with identical tokens. 3. The contract transfers the same token in and out, but applies the fee math, corrupting the internal reserves relative to the actual balance.
prerequisites: Secondary exploit to initialize a ghost pool

potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: None directly unless they interact with the ghost pool

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for identical token swaps
